items.q: Is radioactive decay periodic — does each nucleus decay at regular intervals?
items.correct: No — decay is a random process; half-life is a statistical property of a large population.
items.misconception: Yes — each nucleus 'ticks' at intervals of one half-life until it decays.
items.random_wrong: Yes — half-life is the time between each emission from the source.
items.why: Students treat half-life as a clock for individual atoms instead of a probability-based property of bulk samples.

items.q: Why does increasing the intensity of light below the threshold frequency not eject electrons?
items.correct: Each photon's energy is below the work function; many low-energy photons can't combine to eject an electron.
items.misconception: Enough low-frequency photons should eventually eject an electron if you wait long enough.
items.random_wrong: Low-frequency light doesn't have enough mass to push electrons out of the metal.
items.why: Students think energy is cumulative (more photons = more energy delivered), missing that each electron interacts with one photon at a time in the photoelectric effect.

items.q: Is ultraviolet light visible to humans?
items.correct: No — even though it sits next to violet on the spectrum, its frequency is above the range our eyes detect.
items.misconception: Yes — UV is visible because it's right next to violet.
items.random_wrong: Yes — UV is the colour purple.
items.why: Students assume adjacency on the EM spectrum implies visibility; they don't appreciate the sharp biological cut-off at around 400 nm.

items.q: Force between two 1 μC charges 0.1 m apart in N (k=9e9):
items.why: Exact answer: 0.9. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
